front: Ruler: modernizing, Westernizing tsar of Russia who defeated Charles XII of Sweden to win the Great Northern War
Ruler: became the first Russian ruler to call himself Emperor
Ruler: name shared by three Romanov monarchs of Russia, of whom this one (nicknamed "the Great") is by far the most famous
Ruler: commissioned Vitus Bering's Great Northern Expedition, which mapped much of the Siberian coast and Kamchatka
Event: a power struggle between the Miloslavsky and Naryshkin families in the early years of Peter's reign let his half-sister Sophia (with Vasily Galitzine) incite a streltsy uprising
work: Streltsy Revolts & the Regency of Sophia

front: Ruler: in youth, gained military experience via war games with a "toy army," including exercises at the wooden fortress "Presburg" aided by Franz Zimmerman
work: Streltsy Revolts & the Regency of Sophia

front: Event: broke out in 1698 while Peter was abroad on his Grand Embassy, aiming to replace him with his sister Sophia; he cut the trip short and executed the rebels on return
work: Streltsy Revolts & the Regency of Sophia

front: Ruler: eventually disbanded the streltsy entirely after repeated revolts
work: Streltsy Revolts & the Regency of Sophia

front: Ruler: an earlier revolt against him was suppressed with help from the Scotsman Patrick Gordon
work: Streltsy Revolts & the Regency of Sophia

front: Event: Peter toured Western Europe incognito on this 1697-98 trip, adopting the alias "Pyotr Mikhailov" to conceal his identity

Event: during this trip, Peter worked incognito as a shipbuilder in Zaandam and Amsterdam, staying with his old friend Gerrit Kist and working for Lynst Rogge; the small house he stayed in at Zaandam now bears his name
Event: this tour was publicly/nominally led by the Swiss-born general Franz Lefort, named one of three "Grand Ambassadors"; other members included Alexander Menshikov
Event: this trip failed to secure a military alliance against the Ottoman Empire, despite appeals to William III of England and Holy Roman Emperor Leopold I
Event: during this trip, Peter stayed at Sayes Court, an English estate lent by William III and belonging to diarist John Evelyn, and trashed the place (ruining its hedgerows)
Ruler: astronomer Edmund Halley reportedly pushed him around a garden in a wheelbarrow during his Grand Embassy
Ruler: had an affair with the British actress Letitia Cross during his Grand Embassy
Ruler: observing foreign civil-service structures during his Grand Embassy helped inspire his later Table of Ranks
Event: employed advanced foreign specialists recruited on this trip, including Dr. Nikolaas Bidloo, Dr. Robert Erskine, engineer John Perry, and naval commander Cornelis Cruys
Ruler: met the collector Jacob de Wilde during his Grand Embassy, an encounter depicted in an engraving by de Wilde's daughter Maria
Event: a conspiracy theory held that during this trip its leader was tarred and stuffed in a barrel (or captured and held in Riga) and replaced at home by a German imposter
Event: at a dance during this trip, women wearing whalebone corsets led onlookers to hypothesize their rib cages were on the outside of their bodies
Event: Peter captured this Ottoman fortress, fighting alongside Austria, Venice, and Poland in the Holy League
Ruler: founded a fortress base at Taganrog after his successful Azov campaigns
Ruler: Alexander Menshikov led his "toy army" during the Azov campaigns
Ruler: built a new naval base at Kronstadt; ships built there were later used in his navy's victory at the Battle of Gangut
War: Peter defeated Charles XII of Sweden in this war, gaining Russia parts of the Baltic coast
Battle: Peter's decisive land victory over Charles XII in the Great Northern War
Battle: Peter's navy's victory using ships built at Kronstadt
Ruler: to relieve a Swedish blockade begun by Admiral Gustaf Wattrang near the Aland Islands, ordered 70 galleys to run it
Ruler: his army forced an opposing Swedish force to undertake the Carolean Death March
Ruler: allied with Augustus the Strong of Saxony-Poland against Charles XII, meeting him at the Birzai Conference
Ruler: his foreign policy, influenced by the German adventurer Johann Patkul, led to the war-ending Treaty of Nystad
Ruler: waged a "Persian campaign," remembered as the second of the Russo-Persian Wars
Law: created in 1722, this fourteen-rank meritocratic hierarchy for military and civil service replaced the hereditary mestnichestvo system
work: State & Bureaucratic Reforms: the Table of Ranks

front: Law: under this system, reaching the eighth of fourteen levels granted a commoner hereditary nobility
work: State & Bureaucratic Reforms: the Table of Ranks

front: Ruler: consolidated a growing number of prikazy (executive departments) into twelve executive bodies, the Collegia
work: State & Bureaucratic Reforms: the Table of Ranks

front: Ruler: created the "Ober-procurator"/General Procurer position as the "sovereign's eye" overseeing the Senate and the corruption-hunting fiskaly
work: State & Bureaucratic Reforms: the Table of Ranks

front: Ruler: declaring "money is the artery of war," replaced the estate tax with a poll tax of 70 kopeks per person
work: State & Bureaucratic Reforms: the Table of Ranks

front: Ruler: eliminated the distinction between hereditary and granted lands and mandated primogeniture
work: State & Bureaucratic Reforms: the Table of Ranks

front: Ruler: massively industrialized Russia without relying on foreign capital
work: State & Bureaucratic Reforms: the Table of Ranks

front: Law: this system was later undercut by Empress Anna of Russia's 1731 decrees privileging military ranks over civil ones
work: State & Bureaucratic Reforms: the Table of Ranks

front: Law: this tax on the boyars forced a Westernized appearance by penalizing facial hair
Law: those who paid this tax received a token stamped with an eagle on one side and a lower face on the other, inscribed as a "superfluous burden"; men without one risked a public shave
Law: peasants and clergy were exempt from this tax, which targeted the nobility specifically
Ruler: was inspired to require shaving by the fashions of Louis XIV's French court
Law: Old Believers, who had split from the Russian Orthodox Church over Patriarch Nikon's reforms, mainly paid a special "znak" token to keep their beards under this tax
Law: this policy was mocked in a satirical lubok (folk print), attributed to Vasili Loren, depicting a cat that "farts sweetly"
Institution: after the death of Patriarch Adrian, Peter replaced the Patriarchate with this body, ending the position of Patriarch of Moscow and All Russia
Ruler: installed his hand-picked patriarch/synod head Feofan Prokopovich
Ruler: mocked the Russian Orthodox Church by founding the "All-Drunken Synod of Fools and Jesters," appointing his former tutor as "Prince-Pope" and parodying the sviatki folk custom
Event: Peter founded this city on the Baltic, intended as a "window to the West," on a site captured from Sweden during the Great Northern War
Ruler: named St. Petersburg for his patron saint
Event: this city became Russia's second-largest (after Moscow) and later the capital of the Russian Empire
Event: this city's architects included Domenico Trezzini and Jean-Baptiste Alexandre Le Blond, and it was once home to the Twelve Colleges government system
Ruler: financed the artists Andrew Matveev and John Nitkin to travel and study in Europe, just as he had done during his own Grand Embassy
Ruler: his son's attempt to defect to Austria with Afrosinya ended with Peter having him tortured to death
Event: after this conspiracy against Peter failed, he executed the plotters over his exhumed step-uncle's grave
Ruler: caused a scandal when his affair with the German woman Anna Mons was revealed

Ruler: elevated the African prince Abram Gannibal (per one account, a gift from Sultan Mustafa II) to a position of power as an army engineer; Gannibal was the great-grandfather of the poet Alexander Pushkin
Event: Peter's crackdowns on peasants fleeing to the frontier sparked this rebellion, during which a rebel captured the government-seized Bakhmut salt mines
Event: the urban posad of Astrakhan joined this unsuccessful uprising against Peter
Work: this equestrian statue of Peter by Etienne Falconet stands in St. Petersburg's Senate Square atop a trampled snake, its face designed by Falconet's daughter-in-law Marie-Anne Collot
Work: the Bronze Horseman's base is this 1,500-ton granite monolith, moved on a massive bronze-sphere sledge run
Work: a separate, modern 322-foot-tall statue of Peter, possibly repurposed from a rejected Christopher Columbus monument, depicts him standing atop a stack of ships
Work: Alexander Pushkin's unfinished novel about Peter's court features the protagonist Ibrahim, inspired by Pushkin's own great-grandfather Abram Gannibal
back: The Negro of Peter the Great (Alexander Pushkin)

front: Work: this Pushkin poem centers on a monument to Peter the Great
